---
title: "Vectors and Linear Combinations"
---

## Role

This page introduces the idea that many mathematical objects are best understood as combinations of simpler directions.

## Why It Matters

The idea of a `linear combination` is everywhere:

- basis expansion
- regression models
- feature maps
- signal decomposition
- low-rank approximation

## Intuition

A vector is not just a column of numbers. It can represent:

- a point
- a direction
- a signal
- a state
- a feature representation

A linear combination builds a new vector by scaling and adding known vectors.

## Formal Core

If `v_1, \dots, v_k` are vectors and `a_1, \dots, a_k` are scalars, then

$$
a_1 v_1 + a_2 v_2 + \cdots + a_k v_k
$$

is a linear combination of those vectors.

The `span` of a set of vectors is the collection of all linear combinations formed from them.

## Worked Example

If

$$
v_1 = \begin{bmatrix}1 \\ 0\end{bmatrix},
\qquad
v_2 = \begin{bmatrix}0 \\ 1\end{bmatrix},
$$

then every vector in `\mathbb{R}^2` can be written as

$$
x = a_1 v_1 + a_2 v_2.
$$

This is why the standard basis is so powerful: it spans the whole space.

## Application Lens

In ML, parameter vectors, feature vectors, and embeddings all live in vector spaces. When you understand span, basis, and projection, model behavior becomes much easier to reason about.

## Common Mistakes

- treating vectors as only points, not directions or abstract objects
- confusing a specific representation with the object itself
- forgetting that coordinates depend on a basis
